# Chapter 07

[vl0m2vdp] Flynn rode the solar-lugger into Phoenix like a stowaway in daylight. The pod ran the old interstate at a steady illegal speed, its tires singing on heat-softened asphalt. Outside the glass the desert had been scraped and plated with mirrors and panels, a kingdom of black rectangles drinking the sun. The city rose ahead behind a haze of dust like a mirage that had gotten too rich to vanish.

[tevwjvtq] Cage didn’t take the pod. Cage drove them in a maintenance van with no plates and a blown speaker rattling in the dash, the steering wheel hacked to manual with a splice of cable and a little black box Diesel had built that made the van think it still had a driver. The van smelled of brake fluid and pepper and somebody’s old sweat. Cage kept one hand on the wheel and the other on his knee like he was holding himself down.

[jpjnf20j] Diesel sat in the passenger seat with a folded map made of plastic paper and a cheap screen on her lap that kept going dim in the sun. She wore a cap and dark glasses and had her hair pinned back hard. She watched the roadside cameras as they passed, counting them under her breath, making notes no one asked for. Flynn tried to see what she saw and couldn’t; it was all infrastructure and numbers to him, but she looked at it like it was a living thing with habits.

[af21zyi7] They turned off toward the outer ring where the wealthy kept their air and their silence. The sprawl changed in an hour from tarps and salvage to white walls and scrubbed pavement, to low buildings with no windows and trees fed by pipes. The pods here ran quieter. The people didn’t look up when vehicles passed. Nobody walked unless it was for exercise and even then their eyes were elsewhere.

[aja2e0ar] Diesel had found the place by following the car’s ghost. A private collector’s name didn’t mean anything in a world where names were subscription tiers, but cars left trails—insurance manifests, museum loans, parts orders made through shell accounts, the peculiar appetite for climate control. “Khoury,” Diesel said once, tasting it like grit. “He buys dead things and keeps them cold.” Cage smiled as if he’d heard something holy.

[illyw20t] The compound sat behind a wall that looked like poured sand turned to stone. Cameras were set like seeds along the top, their lenses black and blunt. The gate had no guardhouse, just a thin slot in the concrete where a scanner could read you and decide. Diesel parked them in a service lot across the road under the shade of a solar canopy. The canopy threw a grid of shadow on the hood like a prison.

[o9whi49i] They watched. That was the first job, the simplest and the hardest. Flynn lay in the back of the van with a coil of fiber in his hands and a cheap drone the size of a bird, listening to Cage and Diesel talk in low voices up front. The air conditioner ticked and wheezed. Outside, a landscaping crew moved like ghosts in identical uniforms. Nothing happened. The wall didn’t blink.

[hcbzcyy7] Cage had made it sound like breaking into a museum would be a kind of carnival. Now Flynn saw it was just waiting and counting and trying to imagine the unseen schedule of rich people. The place wasn’t militarized. No gun turrets, no dogs. It didn’t need them. It relied on the fact that nobody wanted the trouble. It relied on the law being a habit.

[seq6826f] Diesel sketched the systems on the plastic paper map. “Perimeter cams,” she said, tapping, “twelve. Thermal in the corners. The roof has lidar. The gate’s tied to a city credential system. Inside, a separate network for the garage. The garage is its own little church.” She said church like she hated it. Cage stared at the wall and didn’t blink.

[dxcejswp] Flynn’s part was inside those wires. Diesel had found a maintenance access point on the city side of the wall, a panel with a lock that was more polite than secure. “You don’t pick it,” she told him. “You become it. You talk to it like you belong.” Flynn nodded like he understood. He had grown up with devices that listened for your voice and asked for your consent. He had never grown up with devices that could be lied to.

[oh1x0dbn] They went back at dusk and walked the sidewalk like anyone else, wearing hats and carrying a bag of tools that looked like a hundred other bags. Cage kept his shoulders loose, casual, like he was out for air. Diesel walked with the steady pace of someone who had done labor her whole life. Flynn felt his heart thumping in his throat every time a pod whispered past.

[al0b71us] At the maintenance panel Diesel knelt and made herself small. Her fingers were quick and clean despite the grease under the nails. She slid a thin blade under the seam and listened, her head cocked as if the concrete might speak. “Cheap,” she murmured. “They pay for the wall and forget the seams.” Cage watched her hands like he wanted to bite them.

[y75fygy2] Flynn plugged his fiber in and the world shifted. The cheap screen in his bag filled with a lattice of permissions and timestamps and device IDs. It was all language, all law, all soft barriers pretending to be hard. He found the camera feed in seconds. He found the thermal sensors. He found the garage network like a bright sealed room inside a house. It was passworded. Of course it was. But the password lived somewhere. Everything lived somewhere.

[qz3phv5k] Diesel kept her eyes on the street while Flynn worked. She didn’t rush him, but her stillness was its own pressure. Cage stood with his hands in his pockets, smiling faintly at nothing. Flynn realized Cage didn’t feel the same fear. Cage had a different hunger moving under his skin, a kind of anticipation that wasn’t about getting caught but about being close to the edge.

[akx96w60] “Twenty minutes,” Cage said later back in the van, like he was setting a prayer. “In and out. No hero shit.” Diesel snorted. “You mean no you shit.” Cage turned his head toward her and the smile was gone for a beat, something sharp and bright in his eyes. Then it returned and he laughed softly like she’d said something sweet.

[8ao8y0pd] Diesel drew the interior from the stolen feeds. The garage was below ground, a climate vault with air scrubbers and humidity control. Rows of cars sat under sheer covers that looked like skin. There were more than fifty. Some were common as dirt once, old trucks and sedans, kept now like relics. Some were impossible, the kind of machines that belonged to rich men’s stories about their grandfathers. Flynn looked at them and felt a strange pity, like animals kept in cages too small to run.

[nst84ty6] The Shelby was there, a long white thing sleeping under its cover, its lines still violent even at rest. Diesel zoomed in on the badge and said nothing for a long time. Cage leaned close to the screen as if it could warm him. “That’s it,” he said finally. His voice had gone low and calm in a way Flynn had learned to distrust. “That’s the door into Mexico.”

[rlk8s6hq] Diesel did the math out loud. The car hadn’t been started in decades. The fuel system would be varnish. The seals would be stiff. The battery would be dead. “We don’t crank it dry,” she said. “We don’t tear the starter apart in a billionaire’s basement.” She listed tools and steps. Oil down the cylinders. Prime the pump. Bypass the immobilizer. Hotwire the ignition without frying the old harness. Her voice was flat, competent, not romantic.

[0n0oplhp] Flynn asked why they couldn’t just win with what they already had. Cage didn’t answer at first. He watched the video feed of the car, the cover barely moving in the conditioned air. “Because this is what they took,” he said. “They took the sound. They took the burning. They put it under glass and called it history.” Diesel glanced at Flynn like: see what I live with.

[aj8xfbbh] Roles came next. Cage wanted three inside. Diesel didn’t argue the number, only the order. “Flynn kills the alarms and opens the doors. I wake the car. You keep watch and carry,” she said. Cage laughed again. “I carry?” “You like carrying,” Diesel said. “You like being the one holding the weight.” Cage’s face softened, almost tender, and it made Flynn feel sick.

[ta8o9io1] Wrench and Birdie would be outside, in the extraction truck a block down, engine running, eyes on the street. Wrench listened in silence while Cage talked. Birdie nodded so hard his cap bobbed, his jaw clenched in excitement. Flynn watched Birdie and saw his own hunger mirrored there—wanting to be chosen, wanting to be part of something that mattered, even if the thing was a knife.

[d5v51j4r] They drove back to the compound with the city behind them and the desert opening up like a mouth. The air cooled a little. The van rattled over cracked pavement and Cage began to talk about Mexico the way old men talked about war. La Carrera del Chile Rojo, five hundred miles of backcountry, checkpoints in villages that pretended not to see, a purse big enough to buy a year of silence. “Invitation only,” he said. “You show up with the wrong car and they laugh and they kill you.”

[m3xsh6kd] Diesel said, “They don’t kill you for the wrong car.” Cage looked at her. “They kill you for disrespect.” Diesel’s hands stayed on her knees, still. “This is disrespect,” she said. Cage shrugged, and in that shrug Flynn felt the last six chapters of him falling into place. Cage didn’t want respect. Cage wanted the moment where rules failed.

[euhc89qp] The rehearsals were held in the boneyard where the dead cars sat in rows like fossils. Diesel marked a rectangle in the sand with cones and broken glass, a stand-in for the garage doors, and timed them with a cheap kitchen timer that ticked loud in the heat. Flynn ran the sequence again and again, plugging into a mock panel, speaking the commands, watching the feed, counting down. Every time he fumbled the cable Cage’s eyes lit up like he’d smelled blood.

[hcyd86t3] At night they practiced moving like shadows. Birdie drove the extraction truck in loops, lights off, stopping on a mark Diesel had set with a piece of reflective tape. Wrench sat in the passenger seat smoking and staring into the darkness. When Flynn climbed in between runs Wrench didn’t look at him. Cage moved around the practice site like a preacher, correcting posture, telling them to breathe, making it all sound like something noble.

[q78ctamy] Diesel kept finding contingencies. “If the garage network trips,” she said, “we abandon the car. If a guard comes in, we leave. If the gate locks, we go through the service hatch and eat the cameras on the way out.” Cage nodded at all of it like he agreed, but Flynn saw the way Cage’s eyes kept returning to the idea of a guard. Not fear. Not caution. Interest.

[dbsy132n] Flynn learned what Diesel meant by waking a car. She showed him on an old junk engine they kept around like a teaching aid. She talked about compression and spark like it was weather. She didn’t romanticize it. “It’s an animal,” she said, “but it’s an animal you built. Respect it or it bites.” Flynn watched her hands and tried to memorize the calm in them.

[jr5qxftd] Cage cornered Flynn by the water tank one afternoon and asked him if he was scared. Flynn said yes because lying felt pointless. Cage nodded like a doctor. “Good,” he said. “Fear means your body’s working. Pods don’t let you feel fear. They sell you calm. Calm is a cage.” He smiled at his own pun and Flynn forced a laugh that sounded wrong.

[h78aiwmu] “Why this car?” Flynn asked. Cage looked out over the boneyard, the sun bleaching everything until the horizon disappeared. “My old man talked about it,” he said. The answer landed heavy because Cage almost never mentioned family. “He said the sound could make you brave,” Cage went on. “He said when it opened up it felt like the world was finally honest.” Cage’s face was blank when he spoke, but his voice had a tremor like a wire under tension.

[iorn4pq6] That night Diesel caught Flynn alone in the shop and handed him a small pouch. Inside were earplugs and a flat ceramic blade and a packet of stimulant gum. “For focus,” she said. Flynn stared at it. “For when you freeze,” Diesel corrected. Her eyes were tired. “Cage is… Cage,” she said. “He’ll do what he does. You do what you do. And you watch Birdie.”

[t7kkddl1] Flynn asked why she stayed. Diesel didn’t answer right away. The shop smelled of gasoline and hot metal. In the corner the rat-rod they’d built in Chapter One sat like a half-remembered dream, all sharp welds and stolen parts. Diesel ran a rag over her hands that only spread the grease. “Because I can’t do this anywhere else,” she said. “Because I’m good at it. Because leaving doesn’t mean clean.” She looked at him hard. “And because if I leave, he’ll take someone worse.”

[stmozr05] The night before the job they ate beans from a pot and nobody talked much. Birdie bounced his knee under the table. Wrench stared into his bowl like he was reading a future there. Cage was bright, joking, generous, clapping Flynn on the shoulder and calling him brother. Flynn felt the warmth of it and hated himself for wanting it.

[j1g7fyu3] After, Cage laid out the gear on a tarp like a surgeon. Gloves. Fiber coil. Diesel’s tool roll. A portable jump pack. Two cans of old fuel they’d bought off a man with dead eyes. Flynn watched Cage’s hands and saw scars under the knuckles, the faint pucker of revival stitches along the wrist. Cage hummed under his breath, happy, as if tomorrow was a holiday.

[mm03wgt2] Flynn lay in his container room with the air conditioner rattling and the desert pressing in. He could hear the others moving in the compound, doors opening and shutting, a laugh cut off. Sleep came close and then fled. He kept seeing the garage in his head: the cold air, the covers like shrouds, the white Shelby waiting. He imagined the wall alarming, the lights snapping on, Cage’s face turning toward a guard with that bright interest. He stared at the ceiling until dawn bled into the cracks.
